reporting_timeframe: Within 24 hours of suspicion or diagnosis
report_to: Local health department (then forwarded to state and CDC)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, suspected/confirmed diagnosis, AFB smear/culture results, chest X-ray findings, drug susceptibility results when available, treatment regimen started
provider_obligations: Report immediately upon clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for culture confirmation
Initiate respiratory isolation and airborne precautions
Obtain sputum AFB smears × 3 (collected 8-24 hours apart)
Begin empiric anti-TB treatment (RIPE: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol)
Ensure patient completes full course of treatment (Directly Observed Therapy preferred)
Report drug resistance when susceptibility results are available
Contact investigation — health department will coordinate, but provider should identify close contacts
notes: All forms of active TB (pulmonary AND extrapulmonary) are reportable in every US state. Report on clinical suspicion, not just confirmed cases. The health department will conduct contact investigations and may assume case management.

reporting_timeframe: Varies by state — many states do NOT require reporting of LTBI
report_to: Local/state health department (if required in your jurisdiction)
what_to_report: Positive TST or IGRA result, patient demographics, risk factors, treatment plan
provider_obligations: IMPORTANT: LTBI reporting requirements vary significantly by state
Some states (e.g., California, New York) require LTBI reporting; many states do NOT
Check your state health department website for specific requirements
Regardless of reporting: treat LTBI to prevent progression to active disease
Preferred LTBI regimens: 3HP (isoniazid + rifapentine weekly × 12 weeks), 4R (rifampin daily × 4 months), 6H or 9H (isoniazid daily × 6 or 9 months)
Rule out active TB before starting LTBI treatment (CXR, symptoms review, sputum if symptomatic)
Monitor for hepatotoxicity: baseline LFTs if risk factors, then monthly if on INH
Ensure treatment completion — untreated LTBI has 5-10% lifetime risk of progression to active TB
notes: This was the user's specific question. Key points: (1) Active TB is ALWAYS reportable. (2) LTBI reporting varies by state. (3) Treatment of LTBI is standard of care regardless of reporting requirements. (4) If the patient was never treated for latent TB, initiating treatment is strongly recommended. (5) Contact your local health department if unsure — they can advise on reporting requirements and may provide treatment support.

reporting_timeframe: Within 24-72 hours (varies by state; 24 hours for acute HIV)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, HIV test results (antibody, antigen, viral load, CD4), stage of disease (acute, chronic, AIDS), risk factors
provider_obligations: Report all newly diagnosed HIV cases (including previously undiagnosed)
Report new AIDS diagnoses (CD4 < 200 or AIDS-defining illness)
Ensure linkage to HIV care (viral suppression is public health priority)
Offer partner notification services (patient can notify or health department can assist)
Discuss PrEP for seronegative partners
Report is CONFIDENTIAL — name-based surveillance is standard in all states
notes: All 50 states and DC require name-based HIV reporting. Reports are handled confidentially. CDC surveillance uses de-identified data. Providers should also report perinatal HIV exposure.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, symptom onset date, HAV IgM result, risk factors (travel, food exposure, MSM, drug use, homelessness)
provider_obligations: Report positive HAV IgM immediately
Identify potential source (travel, food exposure, contacts)
Post-exposure prophylaxis: HAV vaccine or IG for close contacts within 14 days
Exclude food handlers from work until cleared by health department
notes: Outbreaks among people experiencing homelessness and people who use drugs have been increasing. Vaccination is the best prevention. HAV vaccine is now recommended for ALL children aged 12-23 months and for adults at risk.

reporting_timeframe: Within 24-72 hours for acute; chronic reporting varies by state
what_to_report: HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM (for acute), HBeAg, HBV DNA, ALT, risk factors
provider_obligations: Report acute HBV (positive HBsAg + IgM anti-HBc OR clinical syndrome with HBsAg)
Report chronic HBV — many states now require reporting of all HBsAg-positive results
Vaccinate susceptible household and sexual contacts
HBV-positive pregnant women: ensure newborn receives HBV vaccine + HBIG within 12 hours of birth
Screen for hepatocellular carcinoma per guidelines (AFP + ultrasound every 6 months for cirrhosis)
notes: Universal adult HBV vaccination now recommended by ACIP (for all adults 19-59; 60+ with risk factors). Perinatal HBV prevention is critical — all pregnant women should be screened for HBsAg.

reporting_timeframe: Within 72 hours for acute; chronic reporting varies by state
what_to_report: Anti-HCV, HCV RNA, risk factors, genotype if available
provider_obligations: Report new positive HCV RNA results
Distinguish acute from chronic if possible (acute: recent seroconversion, elevated ALT, symptoms within 6 months of exposure)
Link to treatment — direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) cure > 95% of cases
Screen all adults 18+ at least once (USPSTF Grade B recommendation)
Screen all pregnant women during each pregnancy
notes: Universal one-time HCV screening now recommended for all adults 18-79. Baby boomers (born 1945-1965) have the highest prevalence. Treatment with DAAs is curative and cost-effective. Ongoing risk behaviors warrant periodic re-screening.

reporting_timeframe: Within 24 hours (primary, secondary, early latent) to 72 hours (late latent, tertiary)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, stage of syphilis, RPR/VDRL titer, confirmatory test (FTA-ABS or TP-PA), treatment given, sexual contacts
provider_obligations: Report ALL stages: primary, secondary, early latent, late latent, tertiary, congenital
Treat per CDC STI guidelines (Bicillin LA IM for most stages)
Primary/Secondary/Early latent: Benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM × 1
Late latent/Tertiary: Benzathine penicillin G 2.4M units IM weekly × 3 weeks
Neurosyphilis: IV aqueous penicillin G for 10-14 days
Report sexual contacts for partner notification (health department assists)
Screen ALL pregnant women at first prenatal visit; re-screen in third trimester and at delivery in high-prevalence areas
Congenital syphilis: report newborn immediately; requires specialized neonatal evaluation
notes: Syphilis cases are at historic highs in the US (2024). Congenital syphilis has increased dramatically — missed prenatal screening is the primary cause. The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction can occur after treatment (fever, rigors 6-12 hours post-injection — reassure patient). Follow RPR/VDRL titers to confirm treatment response.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, anatomic site tested, test method (NAAT, culture), susceptibility results if culture performed, treatment given
provider_obligations: Report all positive NAAT and culture results
Treat per CDC STI guidelines: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM × 1 (1g IM if weight ≥ 150 kg)
If cephalosporin allergy: Gentamicin 240 mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO
Test of cure: recommended only for pharyngeal gonorrhea (re-test 7-14 days after treatment)
Re-screen in 3 months for reinfection
Expedited partner therapy (EPT) where legally permitted
Concurrent chlamydia treatment: if chlamydia not excluded, treat presumptively
notes: Antimicrobial resistance is a major concern. Culture with susceptibility testing recommended if treatment failure suspected. Pharyngeal gonorrhea is harder to treat — test of cure required. Dual therapy (with azithromycin) is no longer routinely recommended unless chlamydia co-infection suspected.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, anatomic site tested, test method (NAAT), treatment given
provider_obligations: Report all positive results
Treat per CDC STI guidelines: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 7 days (preferred)
Alternative: Azithromycin 1g PO × 1 (less preferred due to lower efficacy for rectal infection)
In pregnancy: Azithromycin 1g PO × 1 (doxycycline contraindicated)
Re-screen in 3 months for reinfection
Expedited partner therapy (EPT) where legally permitted
Screen annually: all sexually active women < 25, older women with risk factors, MSM
notes: Chlamydia is the most commonly reported STI in the US. Most infections are asymptomatic — screening is essential. Doxycycline is now preferred over azithromycin for treatment (better efficacy, especially for rectal infection). Untreated chlamydia can cause PID, ectopic pregnancy, and infertility.

reporting_timeframe: Within 24 hours (may vary; some jurisdictions accept electronic lab reporting)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, test type (PCR, antigen), result, symptom onset date, hospitalization status, vaccination status
provider_obligations: Laboratory reporting of positive results is generally automated through electronic lab reporting (ELR)
Report hospitalized cases and deaths
Prescribe antiviral treatment (Paxlovid) for high-risk patients within 5 days of symptom onset
Recommend updated COVID-19 vaccination per current CDC guidance
Isolation guidance per current CDC recommendations
notes: COVID-19 reporting requirements have evolved. Most jurisdictions rely on electronic lab reporting (ELR) for positive test results. Provider obligations focus on treatment and prevention rather than manual reporting in most settings. Check current state requirements.

reporting_timeframe: IMMEDIATELY — within hours of suspicion (do not wait for lab confirmation)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, symptom onset date, rash onset date, vaccination history, travel history, exposure settings
provider_obligations: Report IMMEDIATELY on clinical suspicion — this is a public health emergency
Isolate patient (airborne precautions — negative pressure room if available)
Obtain measles IgM and measles RNA PCR (throat swab and urine)
Identify all contacts — exposure is defined as being in same airspace up to 2 hours after infectious patient left
Post-exposure prophylaxis for susceptible contacts: MMR vaccine within 72 hours or IG within 6 days
Measles is infectious from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset
notes: Measles is one of the most contagious diseases known (R0 = 12-18). Airborne transmission — can remain infectious in air for up to 2 hours after patient leaves. Even if rare in the US, outbreaks occur from international importation. One unvaccinated case can trigger massive public health response. Report immediately.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, symptom onset date, cough duration, vaccination history, pertussis PCR/culture result, close contacts (especially infants)
provider_obligations: Report suspected cases immediately — do not wait for PCR confirmation
Obtain nasopharyngeal swab for pertussis PCR
Treat with azithromycin (500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2-5) — treatment is most effective if started within first 3 weeks of cough
Post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin for close contacts, especially household contacts and infants < 12 months
Exclude from school/work for 5 days after starting appropriate antibiotic
Ensure Tdap vaccination is up to date for patient and contacts
notes: Pertussis can be fatal in young infants. Maternal Tdap vaccination during each pregnancy (weeks 27-36) is the most effective strategy to prevent neonatal pertussis. Classic 'whoop' may be absent in adults and adolescents — suspect in anyone with cough > 2 weeks. PCR is most sensitive in first 3 weeks of illness.

reporting_timeframe: Within 72 hours of hospitalization or death
what_to_report: Patient demographics, influenza type/subtype, hospitalization details, vaccination status, outcomes
provider_obligations: Report laboratory-confirmed influenza hospitalizations
Report ALL influenza-associated pediatric deaths (< 18 years)
Routine outpatient influenza cases generally do NOT require individual reporting
Start antiviral treatment (oseltamivir) within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients
Recommend annual influenza vaccination
notes: Outpatient influenza is tracked through sentinel surveillance systems, not individual case reports. Individual reporting is required for hospitalizations and all pediatric deaths.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, symptom onset, lesion description/photos (with consent), sexual history/risk factors, vaccination status
provider_obligations: Report suspected cases immediately
Obtain lesion swab for orthopoxvirus PCR
Initiate treatment with tecovirimat (TPOXX) for severe cases or immunocompromised patients
Advise isolation until all lesions have crusted and new skin has formed
Discuss JYNNEOS vaccination for at-risk contacts (post-exposure prophylaxis within 4 days, or up to 14 days for reduced risk)
notes: Mpox became nationally notifiable in 2022. Primary spread through direct skin-to-skin contact. Higher risk among MSM population during outbreaks. JYNNEOS vaccine is available for pre-exposure and post-exposure prophylaxis.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, clinical presentation, specimen source, serogroup if available, vaccination history, close contacts
provider_obligations: Report immediately — public health emergency requiring rapid contact tracing
Initiate empiric antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone IV/IM)
Post-exposure chemoprophylaxis for close contacts within 24 hours: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO × 1 (adults), Rifampin 600 mg BID × 2 days, or Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM × 1
Close contacts include: household members, daycare contacts, anyone with direct exposure to oral secretions in past 7 days
MenACWY and/or MenB vaccination for contacts as appropriate
notes: Meningococcal disease can progress from well to dead within hours. Immediate antibiotic treatment and aggressive supportive care are essential. Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts must be given within 24 hours of case identification. Report immediately — do not wait for culture confirmation.

reporting_timeframe: Within 24-72 hours of positive culture or NAAT
what_to_report: Patient demographics, specimen type, Salmonella serotype/species, onset date, food exposure history, travel history
provider_obligations: Report positive stool culture or NAAT
Most cases are self-limited — antibiotics not routinely needed
Treat with antibiotics if: bacteremia, severe illness, age < 12 months, age > 50, immunocompromised, prosthetic joints/vascular grafts
If antibiotic indicated: fluoroquinolone, azithromycin, or TMP-SMX based on susceptibilities
Obtain travel and food exposure history for outbreak investigation
Exclude food handlers and healthcare/childcare workers until diarrhea resolves (and per local rules)
notes: Salmonella is one of the most common foodborne pathogens. Most cases resolve in 4-7 days. NAAT-positive cases should still be reported even without culture. Health department may request additional stool specimens for whole genome sequencing to detect outbreaks.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, specimen type, Shigella species, onset date, daycare/institutional exposure
provider_obligations: Report positive culture or NAAT
Treat to shorten illness and reduce transmission: ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, or ceftriaxone
NOTE: Antimicrobial resistance is INCREASING — obtain susceptibilities
Exclude from daycare/school/food handling until diarrhea resolved and per local health department guidance
Emphasize hand hygiene — very low infectious dose (10-100 organisms)
notes: Shigella is highly contagious with an extremely low infectious dose. Outbreaks common in childcare settings, institutions, and MSM communities. XDR (extensively drug-resistant) Shigella is an emerging threat.

condition: E. coli O157:H7 / STEC (Shiga toxin-producing E. coli)
what_to_report: Patient demographics, specimen type, Shiga toxin test result, E. coli serotype, onset date, food/animal exposure history
provider_obligations: Report immediately — rapid reporting enables outbreak detection
Monitor for hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS): check CBC, BMP, LDH, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear
DO NOT treat with antibiotics — may INCREASE risk of HUS
Supportive care; avoid anti-motility agents
Hospitalize if signs of HUS (declining platelets, rising creatinine, anemia, schistocytes)
Exclude from childcare/food handling until 2 consecutive negative stool cultures ≥ 24 hours apart
notes: STEC (especially E. coli O157:H7) can cause life-threatening hemolytic uremic syndrome, especially in children < 5 years. Antibiotics are CONTRAINDICATED — they may increase Shiga toxin release. Obtain food and animal exposure history for outbreak investigation.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, Legionella test results (urinary antigen, culture, PCR), onset date, hospitalization status, recent travel, water exposure (hot tubs, fountains, cooling towers, hospital)
provider_obligations: Report all positive Legionella tests immediately
Obtain travel and water exposure history for the 14 days before symptom onset
Treat with azithromycin or fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin preferred for severe disease)
Notify health department about any healthcare-associated cases — triggers facility water investigation
notes: Legionellosis is acquired from contaminated water aerosols (not person-to-person). Clusters in hospitals, hotels, and long-term care facilities require urgent investigation of water systems. Urinary antigen test only detects serogroup 1 (70-80% of cases) — culture or PCR needed for other serogroups.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, parotitis onset date, vaccination history, exposure setting (college, sports team, etc.)
provider_obligations: Report suspected and confirmed cases
Obtain mumps IgM and mumps RT-PCR (buccal swab)
Isolate patient for 5 days after parotitis onset
A third dose of MMR may be recommended during outbreaks for those with 2 prior doses
notes: Mumps outbreaks still occur, often in college/university settings, even among vaccinated individuals. Vaccine effectiveness wanes over time. Complications include orchitis (in post-pubertal males), oophoritis, pancreatitis, and rarely sensorineural hearing loss.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, rash onset date, pregnancy status, rubella IgM result, vaccination history, travel history
provider_obligations: Report immediately — rubella in pregnancy can cause devastating congenital defects
Obtain rubella IgM and IgG
Isolate patient for 7 days after rash onset
If pregnant and rubella immune status unknown: test immediately; if non-immune, provide counseling about exposure risks
Congenital rubella syndrome (CRS): report any infant with compatible findings and maternal rubella exposure
notes: Rubella is rare in the US due to vaccination but still occurs globally. The critical public health concern is congenital rubella syndrome (deafness, cataracts, heart defects, intellectual disability). All women of childbearing age should have documented rubella immunity.

what_to_report: Varicella deaths are nationally notifiable; outbreak reporting of non-fatal cases varies by state
provider_obligations: Report varicella deaths to health department
Many states also require reporting of individual cases or outbreaks
Treat with acyclovir/valacyclovir for: adults, immunocompromised, pregnant women, neonates, complicated cases
Post-exposure: Varicella vaccine within 3-5 days or VZIG for high-risk exposures (immunocompromised, pregnant, neonates)
notes: Varicella case reporting requirements vary significantly by state. Check your local requirements. Varicella deaths are nationally notifiable. Outbreaks in schools/institutions should always be reported.

report_to: Local health department AND state epidemiologist
what_to_report: Patient demographics, animal exposure details, bite site, animal species, capture status, PEP treatment given
provider_obligations: Report any suspected human rabies case IMMEDIATELY
Report animal bites with rabies risk to local animal control / health department
Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP): wound care + rabies immune globulin (RIG) + rabies vaccine series
PEP should be given as soon as possible after exposure — there is no time limit if exposure confirmed
Previously unvaccinated: RIG + vaccine on days 0, 3, 7, 14
Previously vaccinated: vaccine only on days 0 and 3 (no RIG needed)
Do NOT delay PEP while awaiting animal observation results if species is high-risk (bat, raccoon, skunk, fox)
notes: Rabies is nearly 100% fatal once clinical symptoms develop. PEP is virtually 100% effective if given before symptom onset. Bat exposures are the leading cause of human rabies in the US — PEP may be recommended even without a clear bite (bats can inflict wounds that go unnoticed). When in doubt about exposure, call your state health department for consultation.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, clinical presentation (erythema migrans, arthritis, neurologic, cardiac), laboratory results (serology), county of likely exposure
provider_obligations: Report confirmed and probable cases per CDC surveillance case definition
Early localized Lyme: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 10-21 days
Lyme arthritis: Doxycycline 100 mg BID × 28 days
Neurologic Lyme (meningitis, facial palsy): Doxycycline 100-200 mg BID × 14-21 days or Ceftriaxone IV × 14-28 days
Erythema migrans is diagnostic — treat clinically without waiting for serology
Two-tier serology (ELISA then Western blot, or two EIA) for other presentations
notes: Lyme disease is the most common vector-borne disease in the US. Reporting helps track geographic spread of Ixodes tick-borne diseases. County of likely exposure is important for surveillance. Erythema migrans (expanding bull's-eye rash) is a clinical diagnosis — serology may be negative early.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, clinical presentation (fever, neuroinvasive disease), lab results (WNV IgM in serum or CSF, PRNT), county of likely exposure
provider_obligations: Report all confirmed and probable cases
Obtain WNV IgM (serum and CSF if meningitis/encephalitis suspected)
No specific antiviral treatment — supportive care
Neuroinvasive disease (meningitis, encephalitis, acute flaccid paralysis) has significant morbidity/mortality
notes: Most WNV infections are asymptomatic (~80%) or cause mild febrile illness. Neuroinvasive disease occurs in < 1% of infections but can be severe or fatal, especially in elderly and immunocompromised. Peak transmission: June-October.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, Plasmodium species, parasitemia level, travel history (country, dates, chemoprophylaxis used), treatment given
provider_obligations: Report all confirmed malaria cases — nearly all US cases are imported
Identify Plasmodium species urgently (P. falciparum can be fatal)
Obtain thick and thin blood smears AND rapid malaria antigen test
Treat based on species, severity, and chloroquine resistance patterns of travel region
P. falciparum or severe malaria: IV artesunate (available from CDC) is treatment of choice
Contact CDC Malaria Hotline (770-488-7788) for treatment guidance and artesunate access
notes: Malaria can progress rapidly to severe disease and death, especially P. falciparum. This is a medical emergency. CDC maintains a 24/7 Malaria Hotline for treatment consultation. Locally acquired malaria cases (no travel history) require URGENT investigation — report immediately.

what_to_report: Patient demographics, travel history, symptom onset date, lab results (NS1 antigen, IgM/IgG, RT-PCR)
provider_obligations: Report all confirmed and probable cases
Obtain dengue NS1 antigen (first 7 days) and/or IgM (after day 4)
Monitor for warning signs of severe dengue: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, hepatomegaly, rising hematocrit with falling platelets
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs (bleeding risk)
Supportive care with IV fluids for severe cases
notes: Dengue is increasingly reported in the US, including locally acquired cases in Florida, Texas, and other southern states. Second dengue infection with a different serotype carries higher risk of severe/hemorrhagic dengue. Report all cases to support mosquito surveillance and control efforts.

report_to: Local health department AND FBI/law enforcement if bioterrorism suspected
what_to_report: Patient demographics, clinical form (cutaneous, inhalation, GI, injection), exposure history, laboratory results
provider_obligations: Report IMMEDIATELY — anthrax is a CDC Category A bioterrorism agent
Initiate treatment immediately: Ciprofloxacin or doxycycline + 1-2 additional agents for systemic disease
Consider anthrax antitoxin (raxibacumab or obiltoxaximab) for inhalation anthrax
Notify law enforcement if no natural exposure source identified
Post-exposure prophylaxis: Ciprofloxacin or doxycycline × 60 days + 3-dose anthrax vaccine series
notes: Anthrax is rare in the US but is a critical bioterrorism threat. Inhalation anthrax has > 85% mortality without treatment. Classic presentation: widened mediastinum on CXR, hemorrhagic mediastinal lymphadenitis. Cutaneous anthrax (painless black eschar) is less deadly but still requires urgent treatment and reporting.

reporting_timeframe: IMMEDIATELY — single case is a public health emergency
report_to: Local/state health department IMMEDIATELY; state will contact CDC for antitoxin
what_to_report: Patient demographics, clinical presentation, suspected source (food, wound, infant), time course of symptom development
provider_obligations: Report IMMEDIATELY — a single case of foodborne botulism could indicate a contaminated food product
Contact state health department for botulism antitoxin (BAT) — available only from CDC stockpile
Clinical diagnosis is sufficient to initiate treatment — do not wait for lab confirmation
Descending symmetric paralysis (cranial nerves first, then limbs, then respiratory)
Infant botulism: constipation → poor feeding → weak cry → floppy baby — contact California Infant Botulism Program for BabyBIG
notes: Botulism antitoxin must be given as early as possible — effectiveness diminishes with time. CDC provides antitoxin 24/7 via state health departments. Key clinical distinction from Guillain-Barré: botulism is DESCENDING paralysis; GBS is ASCENDING. Foodborne botulism: investigate source immediately to prevent additional cases.

report_to: Local/state health department and infection control
what_to_report: Patient demographics, specimen source, antifungal susceptibility results, healthcare facility, prior healthcare exposures
provider_obligations: Report all confirmed C. auris isolates immediately
Implement contact precautions (gown + gloves) and dedicated equipment
Screen contacts who shared the same room or unit
Treatment: echinocandins (micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin) are first-line
NOTE: C. auris is frequently multidrug-resistant — susceptibility testing is critical
Disinfect with EPA-registered products effective against C. auris (many standard disinfectants are NOT effective)
notes: Candida auris is an emerging, often multidrug-resistant fungal pathogen with high mortality (30-60%). It persists on environmental surfaces and medical equipment. Standard disinfectants may not be effective — chlorine-based products (bleach) are recommended. C. auris is often misidentified by conventional lab methods — MALDI-TOF or molecular testing needed for accurate identification.

report_to: Local/state health department and infection control
what_to_report: Patient demographics, organism identified, carbapenemase gene (if tested), specimen source, healthcare exposures
provider_obligations: Report carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA)
Implement contact precautions
ID-guided antibiotic therapy — ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or cefiderocol depending on mechanism
Screen contacts in healthcare settings per infection control protocols
notes: Carbapenem-resistant organisms are CDC 'Urgent Threat' level. These organisms have limited treatment options and high mortality. Carbapenemase-producing organisms (CPOs) can transfer resistance genes to other bacteria. Report to enable public health tracking and infection control interventions.
